title: Standards Anchoring Ecosystem Audit
scope: Cross-ecosystem — all prior global audits; docs/registers/standards-register.md; docs/compliance/iso27001/

findings.description: The Standards Register External Frameworks table has no Compliance Posture column. ISO 27001 Clause 9.1 requires measurement of ISMS performance — the register documents what standards exist but not whether the organisation considers itself compliant, partially compliant, or non-compliant against each.

tasks.description: Add a 'Compliance Posture' column to the Referenced External Frameworks table in standards-register.md with values: Implemented | Partially Implemented | Planned | Non-Compliant.
Add a 'Compliance Notes' column to capture evidence location (e.g. audit slug, doc path) for each standard's compliance posture claim.

findings.description: Five high-priority external frameworks are absent from the Standards Register: ISO 22301:2019 (BCM), OWASP ASVS v4 (app security verification), SLSA Level 2 (supply-chain), ISO/IEC 42001:2023 (AI management), and CIS Kubernetes Benchmark. Each is substantially implemented but unregistered, leaving no review cadence, owner, or compliance tracking.

tasks.description: Add REF-STD-007 (ISO 22301:2019) to the Standards Register with scope covering the BCP and continuity planning.
Add REF-STD-008 (OWASP ASVS v4) to the Standards Register with scope covering all application security test suites.
Add REF-STD-009 (SLSA Level 2) to the Standards Register with scope covering all CI/CD supply-chain controls.
Add REF-STD-010 (ISO/IEC 42001:2023) to the Standards Register as Planned status with scope covering all AI-assisted workflows.
Add REF-STD-011 (CIS Kubernetes Benchmark) to the Standards Register with scope covering the k3s cluster security posture.

findings.description: The Business Continuity Plan is not anchored to ISO 22301:2019. It is missing three ISO 22301-required elements: Business Impact Analysis (Clause 8.2.2), recovery strategies documented separately from operational procedures (Clause 8.4), and BCP exercise records (Clause 8.5). These gaps are invisible because ISO 22301 is not cited anywhere in the ISMS.

tasks.description: Add ISO 22301:2019 citation to the BCP document header and map the document structure to the relevant ISO 22301 clauses (8.2, 8.4, 8.5).
Add a Business Impact Analysis section to the BCP covering the two critical services (Supabase API, Auth) with RTO/RPO documented against ISO 22301 Clause 8.2.2 requirements.
Add a BCP test/exercise log section to the BCP document. Record the first exercise date as TBD and schedule quarterly.

findings.area: Security test suites — supabase-receptor pgTAP, frontend RLS tests
findings.description: Existing security test suites (pgTAP, RLS.test.ts, Playwright auth tests) have never been mapped to OWASP ASVS v4 verification levels. Without this mapping, security test coverage is asserted but not evidenced against an external verification standard. ISO 27001 Clause 8.3 requires evidence that risk treatments are implemented.

tasks.description: Conduct an OWASP ASVS Level 1 gap analysis against the existing pgTAP security tests (11_security_policies.test.sql, 37_functional_rls_advanced.test.sql) and document the V1–V14 coverage in a new compliance/security-testing.md document.
Add the ASVS Level 1 claim and gap analysis link to the Standards Register compliance notes for REF-STD-008.

findings.description: SHA-pinning has been implemented across frontend CI workflows but the SLSA Level 2 supply-chain framework has never been cited in any audit or standard. Supply-chain security is implemented but has no evidential standard anchor. SLSA Level 2 requires version-controlled build definitions and build provenance — both are present.

tasks.description: Add a SLSA compliance note to the Standards Register (REF-STD-009) documenting current Level 2 status: version-controlled build scripts (✅), SHA-pinned actions (✅), build service not user-run (✅), signed provenance (❌ not yet implemented).

findings.area: k3s/ — RBAC, network policies, pod security
findings.description: Kubernetes security findings (RBAC-01, KUBE-03, ARCH-08, NET-01) across audits 260312-cicd-environments and 260316-terraform-iac-gap are not cited against the CIS Kubernetes Benchmark. The benchmark provides specific, testable controls for each finding area (e.g. CIS 5.2.2 for pod security contexts; CIS 5.3.2 for NetworkPolicies).

tasks.description: Map the k3s security configuration (RBAC, NetworkPolicies, pod security contexts, Falco) to the relevant CIS Kubernetes Benchmark v1.9 controls and document findings in docs/compliance/kubernetes-security.md.

findings.area: Infrastructure — k3s cluster, Longhorn, R2 backup
findings.description: ISO/IEC 27031:2011 (ICT Readiness for Business Continuity) is not referenced anywhere in the ISMS. The ecosystem has meaningful ICT readiness implemented (dual VMs, Longhorn HA, PITR, R2 backup) but no ICT readiness assessment has been conducted against the IRBC framework requirements.

tasks.description: Add an ICT Readiness section to the BCP document referencing ISO/IEC 27031:2011 and documenting the current IRBC posture: dual-VM cluster (✅), PITR backup (✅), R2 cross-region backup (✅), DR test exercise (❌ not yet conducted).

findings.description: The NIST Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF / NIST SP 800-218) is not cited in any audit despite the ecosystem implementing all four of its practice groups: PO (branch protection, security training), PS (SHA-pinning, Vault secrets), PW (code coverage, static analysis), RV (Dependabot, Renovate). Citing SSDF would unify the CI/CD security findings currently spread across four separate audits.

tasks.description: Add REF-STD-012 (NIST SP 800-218 SSDF) to the Standards Register with scope covering all CI/CD pipelines and the secure development lifecycle.
Add a SSDF compliance posture note mapping the four practice groups (PO, PS, PW, RV) to implemented controls and gaps, in the Standards Register compliance notes column.

findings.description: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 (AI Management Systems) was identified in audit 260307-iso27001-ai-gaps as a forward-roadmap standard and deferred. It has never been added to the Standards Register — not even as Planned. Without a registry entry there is no review cadence, no owner, and no transition plan for a standard directly applicable to the team's core AI-assisted development toolchain.

tasks.description: Add REF-STD-010 (ISO/IEC 42001:2023) to the Standards Register as status Planned with scope covering all AI-assisted workflows (Antigravity, agent skills, .agents/ framework) and a next review date of 2027-03-01.

findings.area: Azure resources — Key Vault, storage account
findings.description: The Terraform audit (260316) cited ISO 27001 A.8.9 for IaC findings but stopped short of citing the CIS Azure Foundations Benchmark for cloud-specific controls. The Azure Key Vault network ACL finding (SEC-01) directly maps to CIS Azure 9.x controls. A formal benchmark citation turns observations into scorable compliance gaps.

tasks.description: Add REF-STD-013 (CIS Azure Foundations Benchmark) to the Standards Register with scope covering the Azure Key Vault (K3sUnlock) and storage account (k3sbackups) resources.

findings.description: No prior audit has mapped the test suites to OWASP ASVS verification levels. Without this mapping it cannot be asserted that the system meets any specific security verification level. ISO 27001 Clause 8.2 requires that risk assessment evidence be demonstrable — tests that are not mapped to a standard's requirements produce weak evidence.

tasks.description: Produce an OWASP ASVS L1 self-attestation table in docs/compliance/security-testing.md mapping each ASVS V-chapter (V1–V14) to passed/failed status based on existing pgTAP and Playwright test coverage.

findings.description: vitest-axe and @axe-core/playwright are installed across all three frontend repos but no audit has cited WCAG 2.2 AA as the accessibility target standard. ENG-STD-001 (Frontend Engineering Standards) does not mention WCAG. For a platform serving hospital workers, WCAG 2.2 AA compliance is likely a contractual requirement.

tasks.description: Add REF-STD-014 (WCAG 2.2 AA) to the Standards Register with scope covering all frontend applications.
Add a WCAG 2.2 AA requirement to ENG-STD-001 (Frontend Engineering Standards) specifying that all frontend apps must pass axe-core WCAG 2.2 AA checks in CI.

findings.description: No SLO targets are defined for any ecosystem service and no standard governs what acceptable performance means. ISO/IEC 20000-1:2018 requires SLA/SLO definitions for managed services. Hospital customers will expect uptime and response time commitments.

tasks.description: Add REF-STD-015 (ISO/IEC 20000-1:2018) to the Standards Register as Planned status with scope covering production service delivery.

Define minimum SLO targets for receptor-api.commonbond.au (99.5% monthly availability, <500ms p95) and receptor-auth.commonbond.au (99.9% monthly availability) and document them in docs/compliance/iso27001/operations/service-levels.md.

findings.description: The Grafana/Loki/Prometheus observability stack is planned but OpenTelemetry has not been cited as the collection standard. For the stack to serve as evidential for ISO 27001 A.8.15 (Logging) and A.8.16 (Monitoring), signal collection must be vendor-neutral and standardised.

tasks.description: Add REF-STD-016 (OpenTelemetry CNCF standard) to the Standards Register as Planned status, to be adopted once the Grafana/Loki/Prometheus stack is live.

findings.description: Risk register entry for prompt injection (AI-004 from audit 260307-iso27001-ai-gaps) does not cite the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (LLM01: Prompt Injection). For a team using an agentic AI (Antigravity) with write access to production repositories, citing the LLM OWASP Top 10 adds meaningful precision to the risk entry.

tasks.description: Update the AI prompt injection risk register entry (approx R-011 or adjacent) to cite OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 — LLM01 (Prompt Injection) and LLM06 (Excessive Agency) as the authoritative risk classification.

findings.description: No Statement of Applicability (SoA) cross-references external framework controls. ISO 27001 Clause 6.1.3(c) requires an SoA listing all controls and their applicability. The SoA only maps Annex A controls — it does not record how controls are supplemented by the 10 newly registered frameworks (OWASP ASVS, SLSA, NIST SSDF, CIS Benchmarks). A Stage 1 ISO 27001 auditor will raise an immediate NC if the SoA omits registered but uncited standards.

tasks.description: Locate or create docs/compliance/iso27001/statement-of-applicability.md. Add a 'Supporting External Standards' column mapping each Annex A control to any supplementary external framework references (OWASP ASVS V-chapter, SLSA control, NIST SSDF practice, CIS Benchmark section) where applicable.

findings.description: Risk register entries document treatments but do not cite which standard controls each treatment satisfies. An external auditor cannot trace R-001 (Unauthorised access — treatment: RLS + RBAC) to ISO 27001 A.5.15, OWASP ASVS V4.1, or CIS K8s 5.1 without cross-referencing multiple documents. ISO 27001 Clause 8.3 requires risk treatment outputs be traceable back to controlling requirements.

tasks.description: Add a 'Standard Controls' column to the risk register table. For each risk, list the specific control references that justify the treatment (e.g. 'ISO 27001 A.5.15; OWASP ASVS V4.1; CIS Kubernetes 5.1.6'). Prioritise the top 10 risks by severity.

findings.area: dev-environment/.agents/ — all agent skills, workflows, and scripts
findings.description: The Antigravity agent framework has no governing security standard despite having write access to all production repositories, Vault secret access, and the ability to push directly to protected branches. No prior audit has treated the agent framework as a security boundary. Relevant standards: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 Clause 6.1.2 (AI capability controls), OWASP LLM Top 10 LLM06 (Excessive Agency), ISO 27001 A.8.2 (privileged access rights). The agent's permissions represent the single largest undocumented privileged-access surface in the ecosystem.

tasks.description: Enumerate the Antigravity agent's permissions: repository write-access grants, Vault policy paths, GitHub App permissions, and protected branch bypass rights. Document in docs/compliance/agent-security-boundary.md citing ISO 27001 A.8.2 and OWASP LLM06.

Add a human-approval gate requirement to global-audit.md for any agent action that: (a) pushes directly to a protected branch, (b) creates or modifies GitHub Actions workflows, or (c) updates Vault policies. Document as a control against OWASP LLM06 Excessive Agency.

findings.description: No penetration test has been conducted and no standard governs when one is required. ISO 27001 A.8.8 (management of technical vulnerabilities) and most healthcare customer procurement questionnaires require annual evidence of penetration testing. OWASP Testing Guide v4.2 is the appropriate methodology standard. Without a pentest schedule, the ecosystem will fail procurement due diligence for hospital contracts.

tasks.description: Add REF-STD-017 (OWASP Web Security Testing Guide v4.2) to the Standards Register as Planned with scope covering all production web services at *.commonbond.au.

Create docs/compliance/iso27001/operations/penetration-testing.md defining: scope (all *.commonbond.au endpoints), frequency (annual + pre-launch), methodology (OWASP Testing Guide v4.2), provider type (external), and output artefacts required. Reference ISO 27001 A.8.8.

findings.description: No Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) procedure exists. The OAIC strongly recommends a PIA before deploying systems handling sensitive personal information. The ecosystem stores hospital workforce PII (names, roles, shift data) and is expanding to more hospital customers — each new customer adds new personal data processing. Without a PIA procedure, new customer onboarding creates unassessed privacy risks against REF-STD-002 (Privacy Act), REF-STD-003 (APPs), and REF-STD-006 (OAIC AI guidance). ISO 27001 Clause 6.1.2 requires privacy risks be assessed as part of the ISMS risk process.

tasks.description: Create docs/compliance/privacy-impact-assessment.md defining: when a PIA is required (new data processing activity, new customer, new AI feature, architectural change affecting PII), the PIA template (data flows, risk assessment, mitigation, sign-off), and approver (CEO). Reference OAIC PIA guidance and Privacy Act s.33D.

Conduct and document a retrospective PIA for the current Reactor platform covering: categories of PII processed, hospital customer data flows, Antigravity agent access to PII, and identified risks with mitigations.

findings.description: No supplier security assessment register or procedure exists. ISO 27001 A.5.19–A.5.22 (Information security in supplier relationships) requires formal assessment of suppliers, contractual security requirements, and ongoing monitoring. The ecosystem relies on critical SaaS suppliers — Cloudflare (DNS/WAF/R2), GitHub (code hosting/CI), Supabase cloud (database), Azure (Key Vault/storage), Sentry (error tracking) — with no documented security assessments, no contractual security clauses verified, and no review cadence. A single compromised SaaS provider (e.g. Cloudflare DNS hijack, GitHub Actions supply chain) would affect all production services.

tasks.description: Create docs/compliance/iso27001/supplier-assessment-register.md listing each critical SaaS supplier with: name, service, data classification of data shared, security assessment status (e.g. SOC2/ISO27001 certification check), contractual security clauses, review frequency, and last reviewed date. Reference ISO 27001 A.5.19.

findings.description: No formal change management procedure is anchored to ISO 27001 A.8.32 (change management). Code changes flow through pull requests and CI/CD, but there is no documented definition of what constitutes a 'significant change' requiring formal risk assessment (e.g. schema migrations, new third-party integrations, infrastructure changes). ITIL / ISO/IEC 20000-1 defines change categories (standard, normal, emergency) — without this, all changes are treated equally. An ISO 27001 assessor expects a documented change management procedure for production systems.

tasks.description: Create docs/compliance/iso27001/operations/change-management.md defining: change categories (standard/normal/emergency), approval thresholds, required risk assessment for significant changes (new third parties, schema changes touching PII, infrastructure changes), and rollback procedures. Reference ISO 27001 A.8.32.

findings.description: No data retention or deletion schedule is documented. Australian Privacy Principle 11.2 (APP 11.2) requires personal information to be destroyed or de-identified when no longer needed. CRITICALLY: hospital workforce employment records are subject to a 7-year retention requirement under the Fair Work Act 2009 (s.535) and some state health legislation (e.g. NSW Health Records and Information Privacy Act 2002 requires health service records kept 7 years from last entry, or until age 25 for minors). The ecosystem retains worker shift data, user records, and audit logs with no documented retention periods, no enforcement mechanism, and no legal basis documented per category. Supabase PITR is configured but retention duration is undocumented from a governance perspective.

tasks.description: Create docs/compliance/data-retention-schedule.md defining: each data category (worker PII, shift records, audit logs, application logs, backup data), retention period, legal basis, deletion method, and responsible system. Reference APP 11.2 and ISO 27001 A.8.10 (information deletion).

findings.description: Email security for *.commonbond.au has not been audited against ASD Essential Eight Maturity Level 1, which requires DMARC, DKIM, and SPF records for all domains used for email. ASD Essential Eight is already registered as REF-STD-004 but no audit has ever assessed email security under it. Email impersonation of commonbond.au is a material social engineering risk for a company communicating with hospitals. Current email infrastructure coverage is unknown.

tasks.description: Verify and document the SPF, DKIM, and DMARC configuration for commonbond.au and myjmo.com.au. Record results in docs/compliance/email-security.md and update the Standards Register compliance notes for REF-STD-004 with email security posture. Reference ASD Essential Eight control: 'Configure Microsoft Office macros / Email' and DMARC policy requirement.

findings.description: Vault is used for secrets management but no standard formally governs the secrets management policy: rotation cadence, access audit frequency, emergency revocation procedure, or key lifecycle. ISO 27001 A.8.24 (use of cryptography) and A.8.2 (privileged access rights) apply. CIS Controls v8 Control 4 (Secure Configuration of Enterprise Assets) and NIST SP 800-57 (Key Management Guideline) provide specific secrets management requirements. Without a governing standard, Vault configuration decisions are made ad hoc with no documented audit trail of why rotation periods were chosen.

tasks.description: Create docs/compliance/secrets-management-policy.md referencing ISO 27001 A.8.24 and NIST SP 800-57, documenting: Vault secret rotation cadences by category (database credentials: 90 days, API keys: 180 days, TLS certificates: auto-renew), access audit frequency (quarterly), emergency revocation procedure, and who can approve root token generation.

findings.description: [WITHDRAWN — finding found to be invalid] ISMS Scope document already exists. governance/scope.md is a comprehensive v1.0 document approved by Ryan Ammendolea (CEO) on 2026-03-05, covering services in scope, cloud infrastructure boundaries, out-of-scope exclusions, interested parties, and the full regulatory framework table. Finding closed without action.

Internal audit procedure (assurance/internal-audit.mdx) exists and correctly cites Clause 9.2, using the adversarial review workflow as the documented mechanism. However, three Clause 9.2 compliance gaps remain: (1) IA-2026-01 is referenced in the SoA (control 5.35) as scheduled for 2026-04-15 but does not appear in the audit registry, meaning there is no scheduled-audit evidence trail; (2) no auditor independence declaration is documented — for a sole-founder ISMS, compensating controls must be explicit; (3) the existing audit.md format is not formally labelled as the organisation's Clause 9.2 audit report template. A certification body will request these three artefacts specifically.

tasks.description: Add IA-2026-01 to the audit registry (audit-registry.md and Supabase public.audits) with status 'Scheduled', scope 'Full ISMS — ISO 27001 Clause 9.2 annual audit', and scheduled date 2026-04-15. This creates the required Clause 9.2 evidence trail for the planned internal audit.

Add a section to assurance/internal-audit.mdx documenting auditor independence compensating controls: for a sole-founder ISMS, independence is achieved through (a) the structured adversarial review workflow preventing informal knowledge bias, (b) Antigravity agent as the review instrument (no vested interest in findings), and (c) all audit outputs being committed to public.audits for external review. Reference ISO 27001 Clause 9.2.2(b).

findings.description: Management review procedure exists (governance/management-review.md — a comprehensive 151-line Clause 9.3 agenda template with all required inputs). However: no completed management review record exists. The template has never been executed — there are no minutes, no signed outputs, no action owner assignments, and no dated review cycle. ISO 27001 Clause 9.3.3 requires documented outputs retained per Clause 7.5. A certification body will request the completed review record as primary evidence; a template alone will not suffice. The next review is scheduled for 2027-03-01, meaning a completed 2026 review cycle must be documented before then.

tasks.description: Complete the first management review using the governance/management-review.md agenda template and produce docs/compliance/iso27001/governance/management-review-record-2026-Q1.md containing: meeting date, attendees, completed agenda outputs, action items (owner + due date), and CEO approval signature. This document is retained as Clause 7.5 documented information. Reference ISO 27001 Clause 9.3.

findings.description: No certificate inventory or lifecycle procedure exists. The ecosystem serves production traffic over TLS for *.commonbond.au but has no document recording: which certificates exist, their expiry dates, who manages them (Cloudflare vs Let's Encrypt vs Vault PKI CA), the renewal mechanism, and the alert threshold. Certificate expiry is the most common cause of production incidents globally — an unmonitored expiry causes immediate service unavailability. ISO 27001 A.8.24 requires cryptographic key and certificate management. This gap is especially sharp given the Vault PKI CA used for internal mTLS which has its own root CA expiry.

tasks.description: Create docs/compliance/certificate-inventory.md listing all certificates: domain, issuer, expiry date, renewal mechanism (auto/manual), alert threshold (30 days), and owner. Include Cloudflare-managed wildcard cert, any Let's Encrypt certs, and the Vault internal PKI CA certificate. Reference ISO 27001 A.8.24.

findings.description: [WITHDRAWN — finding found to be invalid] Information Asset Register already exists. operations/asset-register.mdx is a live Supabase-backed register (public.assets) with four asset categories (IA/SA/HW/PA prefixes), annual review cycle, decommission tracking, and an AssetsDashboard Docusaurus component. The SoA records control 5.9 as 'Implemented'. Finding closed without action.

[REJECTED — not applicable at current stage] SoA ⚠️ Confirm markers are expected during the active ISMS build phase. The organisation is using ISO 27001 to prepare for future certification readiness, not undergoing certification audit. SoA confirmation is a pre-certification task, not a development-phase requirement. Finding closed without action.

[REJECTED — not applicable at current stage] NC/CA record creation is a pre-certification maturity requirement. The organisation is using ISO 27001 to guide ISMS development, not operating in a certification audit context. The NC/CA traceability chain (Clause 9.2 → Clause 10.1 → management review) is a future obligation to be established when approaching Stage 1 audit. Finding closed without action.

clarifications.decision: User confirmed: commit directly to main, no PR required for this audit.

The existing docs/registers/standards-register.md is the correct home for all external framework entries. New entries use REF-STD-NNN IDs. ISO 27001 compliance docs reference the register, not house framework entries directly.
Permitted Compliance Posture values: Implemented | Partially Implemented | Planned | Non-Compliant.

implementationPhases.name: Standards Register Schema Enhancement
implementationPhases.rationale: Adding the Compliance Posture column and new framework entries to the Standards Register is the prerequisite for all other phases — it establishes the tracking mechanism.

implementationPhases.name: BCP and Availability Anchoring
implementationPhases.rationale: AVAIL-01 (Critical) is the highest-severity finding requiring a document edit. AVAIL-02 can be addressed in the same BCP edit session.

implementationPhases.name: Security Standard Citations
implementationPhases.rationale: SEC-01, SEC-02, SEC-03 are all standards-citation tasks that reference the new register entries created in Phase 1. Group with INT-02 as all are compliance mapping tasks.

implementationPhases.name: Secure Development Lifecycle Anchoring
implementationPhases.rationale: INT-01 (NIST SSDF) spans all CI/CD repos — register-only task. Combine with PERF-01 and PERF-02 as all are register-addition tasks.

implementationPhases.name: Testability and AI Governance
implementationPhases.rationale: TEST-01, TEST-02, AI-02 require document edits across compliance and engineering standards. Lower urgency — no operational blocker.

implementationPhases.name: Governance, Privacy, and Agent Security
implementationPhases.rationale: PROP-01 (SoA multi-framework mapping), PROP-02 (risk register standard citations), and PROP-05 (PIA procedure) are cross-cutting compliance documentation tasks. PROP-03 (agent security boundary) and PROP-04 (pentest procedure) address structural security governance gaps and can be drafted in parallel.

implementationPhases.name: Supplier, Retention, and Operational Policies
implementationPhases.rationale: PROP-06 (supplier register), PROP-07 (change management), PROP-08 (data retention — 7-year healthcare requirement), PROP-09 (email security), and PROP-10 (secrets management policy) are all operational procedure documents. They can be drafted concurrently as they are independent of each other.
